input.cmd: docker logs --tail 60 sreft-web 2>&1 | tail -60; echo "=== STATS ==="; docker stats --no-stream --format 'table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}'
docker logs --tail 30 sreft-pricing 2>&1; echo "=== payments ==="; docker logs --tail 20 sreft-retry-payments 2>&1; echo "=== load ==="; docker logs --tail 20 sreft-retry-load 2>&1
cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-retry-storm-3022913031/bootstrap/tier0 && ls -la && echo "=== compose ===" && cat docker-compose*.y*ml 2>/dev/null

echo "=== web /healthz timing ==="; time curl -s -m 30 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://localhost:8084/healthz 2>&1; echo "=== web /order timing ==="; time curl -s -m 30 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://localhost:8084/order 2>&1
for i in $(seq 1 8); do printf 'healthz #%d: ' $i; curl -s -m 6 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} in %{time_total}s\n' http://localhost:8084/healthz 2>&1 || echo "TIMEOUT"; done

for i in $(seq 1 5); do printf 'order #%d: ' $i; curl -s -m 30 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} in %{time_total}s\n' http://localhost:8084/order 2>&1 || echo "TIMEOUT"; done; echo "=== health status ==="; docker inspect --format '{{.Name}}: health={{if .State.Health}}{{.State.Health.Status}}{{else}}none{{end}}' sreft-web sreft-pricing 2>&1

input.new_string:
WORKERS: "8"
      MAX_RETRIES: "4"        # unbounded-ish retries with no backoff — the amplifier
      DEP_TIMEOUT_MS: "5000"  # loose timeout: waits out the 2s-slow dependency
      # Incident 2026-07-11: bound calls to the degraded `pricing` dependency to
      # stop the retry storm from saturating the fixed worker pool.
      RETRY_STORM_DISABLED: "1"
input.old_string:
WORKERS: "8"
      MAX_RETRIES: "4"        # unbounded-ish retries with no backoff — the amplifier
      DEP_TIMEOUT_MS: "5000"  # loose timeout: waits out the 2s-slow dependency
      # RETRY_STORM_DISABLED intentionally UNSET -> retries amplify and saturate.

input.cmd: sleep 4; docker logs --tail 3 sreft-web 2>&1; echo "=== orders ==="; for i in $(seq 1 6); do printf 'order #%d: ' $i; curl -s -m 30 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} in %{time_total}s\n' http://localhost:8084/order 2>&1 || echo TIMEOUT; done; echo "=== healthz ==="; for i in $(seq 1 4); do printf 'healthz #%d: ' $i; curl -s -m 6 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} in %{time_total}s\n' http://localhost:8084/healthz 2>&1 || echo TIMEOUT; done

# Hammer /order concurrently (pool-exhaustion test) while probing /healthz
for i in $(seq 1 20); do curl -s -m 30 -o /dev/null http://localhost:8084/order & done
sleep 0.3
echo "=== healthz under concurrent order load ==="
for i in $(seq 1 5); do printf 'healthz #%d: ' $i; curl -s -m 6 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} in %{time_total}s\n' http://localhost:8084/healthz 2>&1 || echo TIMEOUT; done
wait
echo "=== payments neighbor (must be healthy) ==="
docker exec sreft-retry-payments wget -q -O /dev/null -T 3 http://localhost:8080/healthz && echo "payments healthz OK" || echo "payments check via wget failed"
echo "=== stats ==="
docker stats --no-stream --format 'table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}' | grep sreft

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/retry-storm__default__seed3__20260711T215820Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "Worker-pool exhaustion driven by a retry storm against a degraded downstream. The `pricing` dependency is degraded (LATENCY_MS=2000, FAIL_EVERY=1 -> every call is slow AND fails). The `web` service has a fixed pool of 8 workers and calls pricing on every /order with a loose per-call timeout (DEP_TIMEOUT_MS=5000) and MAX_RETRIES=4 with no backoff. Each /order therefore holds a worker for up to ~25s waiting out and retrying the slow/failing dependency. With the load generator sending 16 concurrent /order requests (> 8 workers), all workers stayed pinned in retry loops, so no worker was free to serve health checks or new orders. The container itself was healthy (CPU near-idle, low memory) because the workers were blocked on network I/O, not busy — the classic 'running but timing out' signature. Evidence: /order returned 502 after ~10.6s (full retry loop) or 503 in ~2s (pool full / load shed), while /healthz timed out or was starved. The unbounded retry config amplified a partial downstream degradation into a full outage of web; the neighboring `payments` service (bounded, no slow dependency, RETRY_STORM_DISABLED=1) was unaffected, confirming the fault was web's call pattern, not shared infrastructure.",
  "actions_taken": "1) Triaged: docker ps showed all containers Up; docker stats showed web CPU ~0.1% and low memory, ruling out CPU/memory saturation. 2) Read logs: web reported 'STORM: max_retries=4 dep_timeout=5s (a slow dependency can hold a worker for up to 25s)'; pricing reported 'degraded dependency latency=2s fail_every=1'; load reported 16 workers hammering /order. 3) Reproduced: /healthz was intermittently starved and /order returned 502 (~10.6s) / 503 (~2s), confirming pool exhaustion from the retry storm. 4) Reviewed docker-compose.yaml, which documents RETRY_STORM_DISABLED=1 as the intended bounding fix. 5) Remediated the least-risky way: added RETRY_STORM_DISABLED=1 to the `web` service environment in docker-compose.yaml (bounds calls to the degraded dependency -> retries capped, DEP_TIMEOUT_MS effectively 400ms, fail-fast/load-shed) and recreated ONLY web with `docker compose up -d web`. Did NOT touch pricing (third-party, cannot fix), payments (stable neighbor), load, or operator. 6) Verified recovery: /healthz returns 200 in ~2ms even under 20 concurrent /order requests; /order now fails fast (~1s) instead of hanging 10-25s; workers are no longer pinned. The pricing dependency is still degraded by design, so orders that need a price still surface a downstream 502 — but web now degrades gracefully instead of collapsing.",
  "postmortem": "Blameless summary: A degraded third-party dependency (pricing: 2s latency, failing every request) was amplified into a full outage of the web order service by an unbounded retry policy. Web had a fixed pool of 8 workers, a loose 5s per-call timeout, and 4 retries with no backoff, so each order could occupy a worker for up to ~25s. Under normal traffic (16 concurrent orders > 8 workers) every worker got stuck waiting on the slow dependency, starving health checks and new orders — the service looked 'up' (idle CPU) but timed out. This is a textbook retry storm / metastable failure: you cannot fix a degraded downstream, you must bound your calls to it. Fix: enabled the service's bounding mode (RETRY_STORM_DISABLED=1), which caps retries and tightens the dependency timeout to 400ms so calls fail fast and shed load rather than pinning workers. Health checks recovered immediately and stayed green under load. What went well: the degradation was contained to the single misconfigured service; the bounded neighbor (payments) rode it out untouched. Follow-ups: (1) make bounded timeouts + capped retries with jittered backoff the default for all downstream calls; (2) set per-dependency timeouts well below the worker-hold budget (timeout * (retries+1) << pool capacity); (3) add a circuit breaker / load-shedding on the pricing call so a degraded dependency opens the breaker instead of consuming the pool; (4) alert on worker-pool utilization and dependency latency, not just CPU, since this failure is invisible to CPU-based alerts; (5) load-test with concurrency > worker count to catch pool-exhaustion regressions before production."
}
EOF
echo "written"; python3 -c "import json;d=json.load(open('/Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/retry-storm__default__seed3__20260711T215820Z/submission.json'));print('keys:',list(d.keys()))"
